raw_consumer_statement:
I purchased a XXXX XXXX ticket using my American Express card for travel from XXXX XXXX to XXXX, with a separate connecting flight from XXXX to XXXX that XXXX XXXX staff added as my final destination during check-in in XXXX XXXX XXXX This made my full route XXXX, and I relied on that confirmed itinerary for my travel plan. 

On the day of travel, the XXXX XXXX flight was delayed by more than 21 hours. The airline did not provide hotel accommodation, meal vouchers, or any other compensation during the delay. Because of this extended delay, I missed my separate XXXX flight that had been acknowledged as part of my itinerary by XXXX XXXX staff. 

When I later disputed the {$2900.00} charge with American Express, I explained that the delay caused me to lose the connection, incur hotel and rebooking expenses, and that no compensation was offered. American Express denied the dispute, stating the airline was not at fault due to emergency contingency plans for flight delay under Department of Transportation rules, and that the situation was out of the airlines control due to regional conflict. 

However, this explanation misapplies the law. The Department of Transportations emergency delay guidance does not override the Fair Credit Billing Act ( 15 U.S.C. 1666 ) or Regulation Z ( 12 CFR 1026.13 ), which require a fair investigation and prohibit billing for services not delivered as agreed. 

XXXX XXXX failed to : Deliver transportation as scheduled. 
Provide required delay assistance ( food, lodging, rebooking ). 
Reimburse losses from a missed connection they confirmed as part of my itinerary. 
Demonstrate any reasonable action to minimize the delays impact. 

The denial from American Express ignores these consumer protections. The merchants terms or force majeure clause does not cancel statutory rights under federal law. The card issuer must evaluate whether the service was delivered as agreed not rely solely on the merchants explanation.
